best_for: Public or third-party-consumed APIs where predictable resource-based URLs and HTTP caching semantics matter
avoid_when: Frontend needs to fetch deeply nested/related resources in one round trip — REST tends toward over- or under-fetching there
tradeoffs: Universally understood, cacheable by HTTP infrastructure for free, but often requires multiple round trips for nested data

best_for: Frontend-driven product where different clients (web/mobile) need different slices of the same underlying data, avoiding over-fetching
avoid_when: Small API surface, or a team unfamiliar with resolver-level performance pitfalls (N+1 queries)
tradeoffs: Clients request exactly the fields they need in one round trip; requires discipline around query complexity limits and resolver batching (DataLoader) to avoid N+1

best_for: Full-stack TypeScript monorepo (e.g. Next.js) where the same team owns both client and server and wants end-to-end type safety without codegen
avoid_when: API needs to be consumed by non-TypeScript clients (mobile app in Swift/Kotlin, third-party integrators)
tradeoffs: Zero codegen, full type inference from server to client automatically; effectively locks the API to TypeScript-consuming clients only

best_for: Service-to-service communication inside a microservices architecture where performance and strict contracts matter more than browser-friendliness
avoid_when: Directly consumed by a browser frontend (needs grpc-web proxy) or public third-party API where REST/GraphQL familiarity matters more
tradeoffs: Binary protobuf format is fast and strongly typed across languages, but not natively browser-friendly and less human-debuggable than JSON

best_for: Notifying external systems (Stripe-style) or client-configured endpoints when an event happens, without them polling you
avoid_when: Consumer can't reliably run a public HTTPS endpoint to receive callbacks — offer polling or websockets instead
tradeoffs: Push-based and efficient for the consumer, but requires you to implement retry/backoff and signature verification for the receiver's trust

best_for: Receiving payment/order/auth events from a third-party platform (Stripe, Shopify, Supabase Auth hooks)
avoid_when: Never applicable when integrating with a platform that supports webhooks — always prefer this over polling their API
tradeoffs: Requires signature verification (HMAC) on every inbound webhook and idempotent handling since providers retry on timeout

best_for: Bidirectional, low-latency real-time features: chat, collaborative editing, live cursors, multiplayer state
avoid_when: One-way server-to-client updates only (SSE is simpler), or the feature can tolerate a few seconds of staleness (polling is simpler)
tradeoffs: True bidirectional low-latency channel, but requires managing connection state, reconnection, and horizontal-scaling considerations (sticky sessions or a pub/sub backplane)

best_for: One-way streaming updates from server to client: live notifications, LLM token streaming, progress updates
avoid_when: Client needs to send frequent messages back to the server too — use WebSockets instead
tradeoffs: Simpler than WebSockets (plain HTTP, auto-reconnect built into the browser API), but strictly one-directional

best_for: Real-time-ish updates needed but WebSocket/SSE infrastructure isn't available (older proxies, simple hosting)
avoid_when: Modern hosting supports WebSockets/SSE — those are strictly better when available
tradeoffs: Works almost everywhere HTTP works, but is less efficient than SSE/WebSockets and adds latency/complexity in edge cases

best_for: Simple status checks where near-real-time isn't required (e.g. checking if a background job finished every few seconds)
avoid_when: Update frequency needs to be sub-second, or many clients polling would create meaningful server load — use push instead
tradeoffs: Trivial to implement on both ends, but wastes requests when nothing has changed and adds latency equal to the poll interval

best_for: Long-running server-side work (video processing, report generation, bulk import) where the client shouldn't hold a connection open
avoid_when: Work completes in well under a second — a synchronous response is simpler and sufficient
tradeoffs: Client gets an immediate 202-style acknowledgment and is notified via webhook/websocket when done, avoiding request timeouts on long work

option: Client Polling a Job Status Endpoint
best_for: Long-running work where the client is a simple frontend without its own webhook receiver, checking `GET /jobs/:id` periodically
avoid_when: A true push channel (websocket/SSE) is already established for the client — prefer pushing the completion event instead
tradeoffs: Simple to implement on both sides without extra infrastructure, but has the same staleness/request-volume tradeoffs as any polling pattern

best_for: Public APIs with external consumers who need an explicit, visible contract version they can pin to
avoid_when: Internal-only API where the frontend and backend always deploy together — versioning adds needless ceremony
tradeoffs: Most explicit and cache-friendly versioning style; requires maintaining old versions in parallel until consumers migrate off them

best_for: Internal API where frontend and backend ship from the same repo/deploy together, so there's never a mismatched-version consumer
avoid_when: Any external or third-party consumer exists — they need a stable contract to build against
tradeoffs: Simplest possible approach for internal-only APIs; breaks down the moment an external consumer or a slower-deploying mobile client appears

best_for: Large or frequently-changing datasets (feeds, logs) where offset-based pagination would skip/duplicate rows as data shifts
avoid_when: Dataset is small and static enough that offset pagination's simplicity outweighs cursor's correctness benefit
tradeoffs: Stable under concurrent inserts/deletes since it anchors to a row, not a position; slightly more complex to implement than offset pagination

best_for: Small-to-medium, relatively static datasets (admin tables, reports) where jumping to an arbitrary page number matters to users
avoid_when: High-write-volume data where rows shift between page loads — cursor pagination avoids the resulting skip/duplicate bugs
tradeoffs: Simple to implement and lets users jump to page N directly, but degrades in both correctness and performance on large, actively-written tables

best_for: Client needs to create/update many records in one request (bulk import, multi-select actions in a UI)
avoid_when: Individual items in the batch need independent, granular error handling that the client must react to differently per item — consider per-item status in the response either way
tradeoffs: Reduces round trips dramatically for bulk actions; response should return per-item success/failure, not just an all-or-nothing result

best_for: Payment/order-creation endpoints where a client retry (due to network failure) must not create a duplicate side effect
avoid_when: Endpoint is naturally idempotent already (e.g. a PUT that fully replaces a resource) — no extra key needed there
tradeoffs: Client generates a unique key per logical operation and sends it in a header; server deduplicates retries against that key — essential for anything touching money

best_for: APIs consumed by multiple client types where consistent metadata (pagination info, request id) needs to travel with every response
avoid_when: Extremely simple internal API where the wrapping adds no value and the team prefers bare resource JSON
tradeoffs: Consistent shape makes client-side handling predictable across every endpoint, at the cost of one extra level of nesting to unwrap

best_for: Simple, resource-per-endpoint REST APIs where the resource JSON *is* the whole response, no extra metadata needed
avoid_when: API needs to consistently return pagination/request-tracing metadata across endpoints — use an envelope instead
tradeoffs: Slightly leaner responses and matches plain REST conventions, but ad-hoc if some endpoints later need to attach metadata

best_for: Stateless APIs, especially ones consumed by mobile apps or third-party integrators, or serverless/edge functions that shouldn't hold session state
avoid_when: Traditional server-rendered app where cookie-based sessions are simpler and CSRF protections are already in place
tradeoffs: Stateless and scales horizontally with zero shared session store, but token revocation before expiry requires an extra mechanism (short expiry + refresh tokens, or a revocation list)

best_for: Server-rendered or same-origin SPA where the backend sets the session and the browser handles it automatically
avoid_when: Cross-origin API consumed by a mobile app or third-party client where cookies don't travel naturally
tradeoffs: Browser handles the token for you (no manual header wiring) and HttpOnly mitigates XSS token theft, but requires CSRF protection and doesn't extend to non-browser clients

best_for: Public or metered APIs where you need to allow short bursts but cap sustained request rate per consumer
avoid_when: Internal API where all consumers are trusted and rate limiting only protects against bugs, not abuse — a simpler fixed-window limit may suffice
tradeoffs: Allows natural burstiness (a user clicking fast) while still enforcing a sustained-rate ceiling; needs a shared store (Redis) across multiple server instances

best_for: Simple internal protection against runaway loops/bugs calling an endpoint too often, where burst tolerance isn't a design concern
avoid_when: Public-facing API where consumers legitimately need to burst — token bucket is fairer there
tradeoffs: Trivial to implement and reason about, but allows a burst right at the window boundary (2x the limit across the boundary) that token bucket avoids

best_for: Serverless/edge functions (Vercel, Cloudflare Workers, Supabase Edge Functions) — the deployment model requires this
avoid_when: Long-lived in-memory state or connection pooling is required — use a traditional long-running server process instead, or a connection-pooling proxy (e.g. Supabase's pooler, Cloudflare Hyperdrive)
tradeoffs: No server to manage and scales to zero, but every request re-establishes what a long-running process would keep warm (DB connections especially) unless you use a pooler
